signInInformation.title: Changes to Login Process

description.text:
We have merged the login process for EuroLeagueTV and EuroLeague ID – now a single account lets you access both, plus our Fantasy Challenge game.

**If you previously just had a EuroLeagueTV account, please use the forgot password function to activate your new Euroleague ID account.**

If you previously just had a Euroleague ID account, congratulations! You now also have access to a free tier of videos on EuroLeagueTV.\*

**If you are accessing our Fantasy Challenge via our partner's website, please be informed that our partners do not process, collect nor store any user data of participating users as stated in our Privacy Policy**

signInInformation.asteriskText: *EuroLeagueTV is available worldwide excluding Spain, France, Italy and Monaco.

accountDeletionInfo.text:
Before continuing to delete your EuroLeague ID account, please bear in mind the following:

If you play the Euroleague Fantasy Challenge, you may want to delete your team there first, as it will be more difficult to do that if your account no longer exists. You can do that at [euroleaguefantasy.euroleague.net/en/account](http://euroleaguefantasy.euroleague.net/en/account).

club.info: Valencia Basket is back in the EuroLeague and hosting games at its bran-new Roig Arena. Founded in 1986, Valencia claimed its first trophy, the Copa del Rey, in 1998. Soon the then-named Pamesa Valencia became a regular in the Spanish playoffs. In Europe, Valencia reached the Saporta Cup final twice, but lost in 1999 to Benetton Treviso and in 2002 to Montepaschi Siena. Everything changed for Valencia in the 2002-03 season. First came the EuroCup crown as it swept Krka Novo Mesto in the finals led by MVP Dejan Tomasevic. Valencia then climbed to the Spanish League finals before losing to Barcelona. In its debut EuroLeague season in 2003-04, Valencia reached the Top 16 and came within one win of the Final Four. A second EuroCup title came in 2010, as Valencia downed ALBA Berlin in the championship game with Matt Nielsen, Nando De Colo and Victor Claver as its main pillars. A year later, Valencia reached the EuroLeague Playoffs and lost to Real Madrid. Two more EuroCup championships came in 2014 and 2019. Valencia won its first Spanish League trophy in 2017, led by Bojan Dubljevic, Sam Van Rossom, Fernando San Emeterio and Rafa Martinez. Last season, Valencia reached the EuroCup Semifinals and the Spanish League finals. With an improved arena and a great fan base, Valencia is one of the teams to follow closely in the 2025-26 EuroLeague.

heroMedia.title: Real Madrid outclasses Valencia with elite ball control
heroMedia.summary: Real commits just 5 turnovers and buries 16 threes to secure another home win in its push for playoff positioning

content.text:
Having already faced each other four times this season across all competitions, neither Real Madrid nor Valencia Basket had anything to hide when taking to the Movistar Arena court on Thursday night. With all cards on the table, the hosts pulled off a masterclass in how to protect the ball against an opponent that boasts one of the most aggressive defenses in the EuroLeague.

Real Madrid finished the high-paced affair with 25 assists and only 5 turnovers, dictating the tempo for the majority of a game that they had exactly where they wanted after the break. The two teams traded blows in the first quarter, but Scariolo’s side exploded for 31 points in the second for a 51-43 lead at halftime.

*Los Blancos* went on to open the second half with a 16-5 run that allowed them to pull away, 67-48. From that point on, Real Madrid just made sure to maintain a double-digit lead before finishing the game off by holding Valencia to 14 points in the fourth. Having clinched a 96-79 victory, the hosts also claimed the head-to-head advantage against the visitors, with both teams currently boasting 20-11 records.

“The key was what we did offensively, deciding when to run, when not to run, which kind of set [play] to run,” Scariolo told EuroLeague TV after the game. “The control of the ball that we had, we had just 5 turnovers versus 25 assists. That’s really, really huge.”

Real Madrid’s bigs did a great job containing their Valencia counterparts, with the visitors making only 18 of their 45 two-point attempts. The hosts also managed to halt Valencia’s team play as Pedro Martinez’s side struggled to circulate the ball, finishing the game with 16 assists – 4 shy of their season average.

Meanwhile, on the other side of the floor, 10 players netted at least 6 points, with Mario Hezonja (16), Gabriel Deck (14) and Trey Lyles (12) hitting double digits. A solid 45.7% from beyond the arc was also key to the win, with Real Madrid going 16-for-35 from deep as eight players drilled at least one triple.

“We implemented the ideas and the changes that we had decided to do so that we play better against this type of opponents, that thanks to their talent and the way they play, they play a bit different than all the opponents that we face,” Scariolo said in the post-game press conference.

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by Real Madrid Basketball (@realmadridbasket)

For yet another game, Real Madrid did what it does best this term by protecting its home court. Having lost just one of their 16 games at Movistar Arena, it aims for a top-four spot that would automatically secure home-court advantage in the playoffs.

“It’s very important for us [to be in the top four],” Andres Feliz told EuroLeague TV. “It’s one of the goals. Especially playing at home is very important. From the beginning of the season, playing away has been difficult for us. We just have to improve on that end and I think we’re becoming a better team every day, so we’ve got to be proud of ourselves.”

Twelve teams have grabbed more road victories than Real Madrid this season, with performances away from home being its Achilles' heel thus far. Should Scariolo’s team gradually manage to replicate such no-nonsense outings on the road, it will have taken a huge step towards the ultimate goal – a highly coveted return to the Final Four.

articleMetadata.title: Real Madrid outclasses Valencia with elite ball control
articleMetadata.description: Real Madrid defeated Valencia 96–79 in Round 31 behind exceptional ball security, finishing with 25 assists to just five turnovers. Scariolo’s squad broke the game open with a 31 point second quarter and a 16–5 burst after halftime. The bigs controlled the paint, ten players scored at least six points, and Madrid hit 16 triples while limiting Valencia’s ball movement. With a 15–1 home record, Real continues its push for a top four finish and home court advantage in the playoffs.

heroMedia.title: Stats review: Turning up the pressure
heroMedia.summary: Sportradar's Matt Kamalsky dives into the defensive numbers

content.text:
With eight games remaining in the EuroLeague regular season, the early separation found by the teams in the playoff picture has nearly disappeared as the team in ninth place now sits only one game clear of the team in twelfth. While the competitiveness of the league has evolved over time, so too have some of the strategies teams are using to find an edge.

One of the less glaring examples of that is the application of full-court pressure. During the 2015-16 season, the average team sent defenders to guard the ball in the backcourt only 5.1% of the time with many teams only pressing to foul and only three teams pressing on more than 10% of the possessions they faced. This season, 10% is the EuroLeague average with eight teams eclipsing that mark and two teams pressing on over 22% of the possessions they face.

Some of that can be traced to intent. Many of the teams pressing the most a decade ago were hunting deflections to generate turnovers. There was a reluctance for many years to apply pressure beyond half court as a result – it felt risky to give the caliber of guards at this level a running start if things went wrong. However, coaches have found a comfort level using pressure more passively to disrupt tempo, try to get the ball out of certain players' hands in early offense, or simply leverage their depth instead of aggressively trying to force mistakes.

The table above lists the teams picking up full court most frequently this season. For reference, the league average for turnover percentage sits at just over 14% for all possessions. This group represents teams from across the standings, all finding varying degrees of success and implementing their pressure in different ways.

[Paris Basketball](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/en/euroleague/teams/paris-basketball/prs/) and [Valencia Basket](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/en/euroleague/teams/valencia-basket/pam/) are the two teams picking up the ball over the midcourt line most frequently this season. While Paris has posted similar defensive efficiency numbers whether pressing or not, Valencia has been harder to score on when pressing, often burning time off the shot clock and only having their press broken for transition opportunities 5% of the time – a tiny, impressive number at any level of basketball. Valencia does a stellar job eliminating those outcomes, largely mitigating the risk that made coaches reluctant to apply pressure a decade ago.

While EuroLeague teams typically don't rely on the wide variety of press schemes often seen at lower levels, an occasional trapping zone still pops up here and there. Even without as many drastic variations, coaches create variety in other ways. Some teams deploy specific defenders to hound the ball without committing a second defender, others focus on disrupting the inbound pass, and some, like [Crvena Zvezda Meridianbet Belgrade](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/en/euroleague/teams/crvena-zvezda-meridianbet-belgrade/red/), adjust their pressing frequency from game to game based on their scouting report.

[Panathinaikos AKTOR Athens](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/teams/panathinaikos-aktor-athens/pan/) is perhaps the best example of a team trapping to force mistakes this season, as they have come up with quite a few deflections springing timely traps off the inbound pass or just over half court. Generating a turnover on around a quarter of the possessions they press is an impressive result that makes a couple of extra run-outs here or there a minor footnote.

As the season begins to wind down, the little things teams are doing continue to add up and figure to play a role in who gets into the field and who keeps home-court advantage.

articleMetadata.title: Stats review: Turning up the pressure
articleMetadata.description: With eight games remaining in the EuroLeague regular season, the early separation found by the teams in the playoff picture has nearly disappeared as the team in ninth place now sits only one game clear of the team in twelfth. While the competitiveness of the league has evolved over time, so too have some of the strategies teams are using to find an edge.

heroMedia.title: Valencia survives late Zalgiris rally to stay second
heroMedia.summary: Brancou Badio’s clutch jumper ends a 15-year drought at home against the Lithuanian side

content.text:
For the first time since January 19, 2011, [Valencia Basket](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/teams/valencia-basket/pam/) defeated [Zalgiris Kaunas](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/teams/zalgiris-kaunas/zal/) on home soil. And it did so the hard way.

When a struggling [Sylvain Francisco](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/sylvain-francisco/007975/) buried his third three-pointer of the night, tying the game at 87-87 with under a minute remaining, Roig Arena held its breath. The moment felt hauntingly familiar — a painful flashback to Valencia’s recent Copa del Rey semifinal loss to Real Madrid, when Mario Hezonja led a late comeback to stun the hosts.

Zalgiris, which trailed by 12 midway through the fourth quarter, had clawed back with back-to-back threes by [Dustin Sleva](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/dustin-sleva/011927/) and Francisco, resetting the contest at its most critical point.

This time, however, the ending was different. [Brancou Badio](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/brancou-badio/007527/) took command of the next possession, calmly rising for a tough mid-range jumper that restored Valencia’s lead and sent the fans to their feet.

content.title: Valencia Basket-Zalgiris Kaunas | Round 30 Highlights | 2025-26 EuroLeague

content.text:
“It was a very difficult game,” admitted coach Pedro Martinez afterward. “When they came back and tied the game with Francisco’s three, it was a very tough moment for us mentally, but we played with good personality. The team showed great mentality to suffer; we knew we had to struggle to get this win, so I want to congratulate them for that effort.”

Zalgiris still had a last chance to complete the comeback, but Francisco’s next attempt — another three-pointer with Valencia ahead 89–87 — missed everything, handing possession back to the hosts. [Jean Montero](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/jean-montero/010042/) secured the ball and drew a foul to seal the victory at the line.

“I'm very happy with our work,” Martinez added. “Jean Montero was clever at the end, waiting for the foul. And I’d also like to highlight Badio and the shot he made. It’s a good shot for him, but not an easy one. After that, we played very good team defense. Francisco’s shot looked bad, but that’s because of our defense, and I’m happy for that.”

Montero’s two free throws set the final score at 91–87, giving Valencia its fourth straight victory and its 20th win of the EuroLeague season — a milestone never before reached in the club’s history. The win consolidates the team's grip on second place and keeps postseason dreams very much alive in La Fonteta.

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by Valencia Basket (@valenciabasket)

“I mean, it's exciting,” Omari Moore said, smiling when asked about Valencia’s run. “This is my first year in EuroLeague, but we still have a lot left to go. So, we’re just focusing on it game by game.”

Moore led Valencia in scoring with 16 points and earned special praise from Martinez in the postgame press conference for his composure and leadership down the stretch, especially during Zalgiris’s comeback attempt.

“It definitely was a tough game,” Moore later reflected. “Zalgiris has a really good team. They’ve got one of the MVP frontrunners over there. I thought we did a good job staying composed throughout all 40 minutes, even when they made their runs.”

[Nate Reuvers](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/nathan-reuvers/012738/) also stood out, contributing 12 points on 5-for-5 shooting from the field. His performance matched Moore’s PIR of 18, once again showcasing Valencia’s balance and efficiency.

“It’s great. We’re in a great position right now,” Reuvers said. “But as you know, the standings are really tight, and every game it gets even tighter.”

Valencia will now look to extend its winning streak on the road, carrying the confidence of a team that’s learning to close out tough games — and rewriting a bit of history along the way.

articleMetadata.title: Valencia survives late Zalgiris rally to stay second
articleMetadata.description: Valencia Basket held off a furious late comeback by Zalgiris Kaunas to win 91–87 at Roig Arena, earning its first home victory over the Lithuanian club since 2011.

heroMedia.title: Valencia extends center Jaime Pradilla
heroMedia.summary: The 25-year-old big man is in his sixth season with the club

content.text:
Valencia Basket announced a new deal with [Jaime Pradilla](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/jaime-pradilla/010393/), who extended his contract until the end of the 2027-28 campaign. Pradilla (2.02 meters, 25 years old) joined Valencia in 2020 from his hometown club Basket Zaragoza 2002 and is in his sixth season with the club, four of them in the EuroLeague.

He is averaging 6.8 points on 63.2% two-point shooting and 37.0% from downtown, 3.8 rebounds and a PIR of 9.3. In 107 EuroLeague games with Valencia, Pradilla averages 4.4 points and 2.8 rebounds.

𝘾𝙤𝙢𝙗𝙞𝙣𝙖𝙘𝙞𝙤́𝙣 𝙜𝙚𝙣𝙞𝙖𝙡... 𝙋𝙧𝙖𝙙𝙞 2⃣0⃣2⃣8⃣ 🧡

Cas 👉 Jaime Pradilla amplía su contrato con Valencia Basket hasta 2028https://t.co/nUnyJY3UcA

Val 👉 Jaime Pradilla amplia el seu contracte amb Valencia Basket fins a 2028https://t.co/I2MHxCOOeB

Eng 👉 Jaime… pic.twitter.com/uXHA6O9uKk

— Valencia Basket Club (@valenciabasket) February 27, 2026

Last season, he helped Valencia reach the Spanish League Finals and the BKT EuroCup semifinals. Pradilla has also been a member of the Spanish national team and helped it win a gold medal at EuroBasket 2022. He also took part in the 2024 Olympic Games with Spain in Paris, France, and won two more gold medals at the 2016 U16 European Championships and 2019 U18 European Championships.

heroMedia.title: Record-breaking first half helps Valencia bounce back against Baskonia
heroMedia.summary: Pedro Martinez's team shattered several EuroLeague records at Buesa Arena

linkedIds.text: Kosner Baskonia Vitoria-Gasteiz v Valencia Basket (2026-02-26T19:30:00.000Z)

content.text:
[Valencia Basket](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/teams/valencia-basket/pam/) was on a mission at Buesa Arena as it faced [Kosner Baskonia Vitoria-Gasteiz](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/teams/baskonia-vitoria-gasteiz/bas/) in Round 29. 

The previous weekend, Baskonia lifted the Spanish Cup trophy at Roig Arena after three big wins in as many days. Valencia suffered a heartbreaking semifinal loss to Real Madrid, but it was ready to bounce back. Baskonia fans celebrated before the game as their team presented the trophy – the club's first Spanish Cup in 17 years – to the Buesa Arena faithful.

It had been a long week for Baskonia, full of intense action and a major celebration. Even so, Valencia delivered its best performance, breaking several EuroLeague records that had stood since the beginning of the competition with an outstanding first half for the ages on its way to a 79-108 victory.

content.title: Kosner Baskonia Vitoria-Gasteiz-Valencia Basket | Round 29 Highlights | 2025-26 EuroLeague

content.text:
Valencia led 36-68 at halftime. The 32-point margin set a new EuroLeague record for a road team at halftime. FC Barcelona held the previous record with a 31-point halftime lead (19-50) against Fenerbahce Beko Istanbul on February 13, 2013. Barcelona went on to win that game 60-99. But there was more.

Valencia broke two additional records in its iconic first half. Its 68 points were the most ever scored by a team in a EuroLeague first half. [Neal Sako](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/neal-sako/010545/) capped the historic effort with a put-back dunk seconds before the break. Virtus Bologna previously held the record with 66 first-half points against Cibona Zagreb on October 25, 2000. Virtus won that game 116–88 and later became the original EuroLeague champion, defeating Baskonia 3-2 in the five-game finals.

It was also the highest scoring total by a road team in any half in EuroLeague history. Only Real Madrid has scored more points in any half – 70 in the second half of a 116–94 home victory over Ovarense Aerosoles on December 20, 2000. That remains one of the oldest team records in competition history.

"I think we needed this win and we worked for it," [Jean Montero](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/en/euroleague/players/jean-montero/010042/) said after the game. "We stayed true to our game, our philosophy – running, playing aggressive defense, and obviously shooting a lot of threes."

Valencia did not slow down after halftime, extending its lead to 58–93 after three quarters. It marked the fifth-highest scoring total through three quarters in EuroLeague history and the second-highest by a road team. Panathinaikos AKTOR Athens scored 94 points in the first 30 minutes of an 83–123 road victory over Chorale Roanne on November 21, 2007.

19 en @EuroLeague, familia 🧡

El jueves que viene volvemos, en casa, en un partido muy importante para seguir mirando arriba en la máxima competición europea.

Tocará empujar como vosotros sabéis 🔥

Bona nit 🧡 pic.twitter.com/0UID3poIDS

— Valencia Basket Club (@valenciabasket) February 26, 2026

Baskonia trimmed the deficit in the fourth quarter but could not prevent Valencia from setting a new club scoring record in the competition with 108 points. The previous record was 107 in a home win over Anadolu Efes Istanbul on February 18, 2004. Montero led the way with 20 points, 7 rebounds and 6 assists. [Braxton Key](https://www.euroleaguebasketball.net/euroleague/players/braxton-key/014168/) set new career highs with 14 points and a PIR of 20. All 12 Valencia players scored at least 4 points.

"We had a heartbreaker against Real Madrid. We practiced hard this week, ready to get back to playing, ready to get back to competition. So it was a good win for us, for sure," Key said after the game. 

"We've got to get every win we can right now. We knew it was going to be a tough game – they just won Copa – so we knew coming in we had to be focused and ready to go. We’ve got to stack wins right now. But it starts each day in practice. Day by day, we’ll keep it going and hopefully carry that momentum into the playoffs."

Valencia plays three of its next four games at home, beginning with its Round 30 showdown against Zalgiris Kaunas on Thursday, March 5.

"It's a very balanced competition," coach Pedro Martinez said after the game. "We're in a good position now, but nine games is a lot. If we lose two in a row, we could drop six or seven spots. We have to stay calm and be ready for the next game."

linkedId.text: Kosner Baskonia Vitoria-Gasteiz v Valencia Basket (2026-02-26T19:30:00.000Z)

articleMetadata.title: Record-breaking first half helps Valencia bounce back against Baskonia
articleMetadata.description: Valencia Basket was on a mission at Buesa Arena as it faced Kosner Baskonia Vitoria-Gasteiz in Round 29. Valencia delivered its best performance, breaking several EuroLeague records that had stood since the beginning of the competition with an outstanding first half for the ages on its way to a 79-108 victory.

heroMedia.title: Phase 2 general public tickets for the 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens presented by Etihad sell out

content.text:
The extraordinary demand for the 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens Presented by Etihad shows no signs of slowing down, as all tickets made available during Phase 2 of public sales have officially sold out.

When Phase 2 opened on February 26 at 10:00 CET, fans once again responded in massive numbers. Following Phase 1, which saw more than one million fans connect to the ticketing platform and resulted in a complete sellout, the second allocation was also quickly exhausted, reaffirming the immense appeal of European basketball’s premier event.

The overwhelming global interest highlights the unique atmosphere and significance of the Final Four, with fans from across Europe and beyond eager to secure their place in Athens for the season’s ultimate showdown.

Fans who were unable to purchase tickets during General public tickets sale Phase 2 will still have an additional opportunity on February 26 as VIP tickets will be available starting at 14:00 CET.

Fans who were unable to secure tickets during Phase 1 will still have multiple opportunities to attend European basketball’s premier event. The second public ticket sales phase will open on February 26 at 10:00 CET. The third public ticket sales phase will open on March 17 at 10:00 CET, while the final sales phase will begin on April 20 at 10:00 CEST, coinciding with the conclusion of the Regular Season as teams continue their pursuit of a Final Four berth.

Each remaining phase will feature a limited allocation across multiple price categories, and high demand is expected once again.

All ticketing details for the 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens Presented by Etihad are available at [https://euroleaguef4tickets.com/en/](https://euroleaguef4tickets.com/en/).

heroMedia.title: Power rankings: After the cups have gone
heroMedia.summary: Our experts offer up a suggestion for every team through 28 rounds

content.text:
Welcome to the latest edition of the EuroLeague Power Rankings. The list is the brainchild of [EuroLeague.net](http://EuroLeague.net)'s team of experts, which is made up of Geoff Gillingham, Javier Gancedo, Frankie Sachs and Igor Petrinovic. The rankings are not a prediction of what the final standings will look like, but rather where all 20 teams stand – in their humble opinions – today, after Round 28.

 

### 1. Fenerbahce Beko Istanbul (20-7)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Besiktas on Sunday to win the Turkish Cup

**The question we are asking:** Is Fenerbahce aware that not team to finish the regular season in first place under the current format has won the championship?

 

### 2. Olympiacos Piraeus (18-9)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Panathinaikos in the Greek Cup final on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** Do we need to start worrying about the health of the Reds’ stars?

 

### 3. Real Madrid (17-11)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Baskonia in the Spanish Cup final on Sunday

**The question we are asking:** Can *Los Blancos* improve its defense to become a top contender?

### 4. Panathinaikos AKTOR Athens (16-12)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Olympiacos to win the Greek Cup on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** Will the addition of Nigel Hayes-Davis be enough to put the Greens over the top?

 

### 5. Valencia Basket (18-10)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Real Madrid in the Spanish Cup semifinal on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** How will Valencia shake its disappointment from not winning the Spanish Cup at home? 

 

### 6. FC Barcelona (17-11)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Barcelona in the Spanish Cup semifinal on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** With Punter back in action, can Barça improve its consistency and stay in the top-six race?

 

### 7. Zalgiris Kaunas (16-12)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Rytas Vilnius in the Lithuanian Cup final on Sunday

**The question we are asking:** With six home games remaining and good momentum, is a top-six finish a realistic goal?

### 8. AS Monaco (16-12)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Le Mans to win the French Leaders Cup on Sunday

**The question we are asking:** After a turbulent stretch on and off the court, can this trophy galvanize Monaco and stabilize its EuroLeague push?

 

### 9. Hapoel IBI Tel Aviv (16-11)

**What they did last week:** Went 3-0 in the Israeli League

**The question we are asking:** Is Hapoel’s recent slide just a bump in the road or a sign that the team peaked too early?

 

### 10. Crvena Zvezda Meridianbet Belgrade (16-12)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Mega Belgrade to win the Serbian Cup on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** Is Jordan Nwora the star who can carry Zvezda to new heights? 

 

### 11. Dubai Basketball (14-14)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Bosna Sarajevo in the ABA League

**The question we are asking:** Now that Dzanan Musa is back and in shape, can Dubai win enough games to make it to the Play-In Showdown?

### 12. Maccabi Rapyd Tel Aviv (13-15)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Bnei Herzliya to win the Israeli Cup on Thursday

**The question we are asking:** Is there enough time left in the season for Maccabi to climb to 10th place?

 

### 13. EA7 Emporio Armani Milan (14-14)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Tortona to win the Italian Cup on Sunday

**The question we are asking:** Can Milan translate domestic momentum into EuroLeague consistency and revive its play-in hopes?

 

### 14. FC Bayern Munich (12-16)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Bamberg in German Cup semifinals on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** What would this team have looked like with a full season under Coach Pesic?

 

### 15. Anadolu Efes Istanbul (9-19)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Besiktas in the Turkish Cup semifinal on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** Is Efes destined to become a spoiler for a few teams in the playoff race?

### 16. Virtus Bologna (12-16)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Tortona in the Italian Cup semifinals on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** What adjustments can Dusko Ivanovic make to halt Virtus’s slide – especially away from home?

 

### 17. Partizan Mozzart Bet Belgrade (9-19)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Mega Belgrade in the Serbian Cup semifinals on Saturday

**The question we are asking:** Can Joan Penarroya realign the locker room and restore Partizan’s competitive edge?

 

### 18. Paris Basketball (9-18)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Bourg in the French Leader’s Cup quarterfinals on Friday

**The question we are asking:** What will it take for Paris to recapture its early-season identity and get Nadir Hifi back to his best?

 

### 19. Kosner Baskonia Vitoria-Gasteiz (9-19)

**What they did last week:** Defeated Real Madrid in the Spanish Cup final on Sunday

**The question we are asking:** After its Spanish Cup success and strong home performances, can Baskonia start winning consistently on the road?

 

### 20. LDLC ASVEL Villeurbanne (7-21)

**What they did last week:** Lost to Monaco in the French Cup quarterfinals on Tuesday

**The question we are asking:** With the postseason out of reach, which players are auditioning for their EuroLeague futures?

heroMedia.title: 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens presented by Etihad – Ticket sales Phase 2 to start on February 26
heroMedia.summary: Phase 1 general public ticket sale attracted over 1 million fans worldwide

content.text:
Ticket sales for the 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens presented by Etihad will enter their next chapter on February 26 at 10:00 CET, as Phase 2 opens to the general public.

The launch of Phase 2 coincides with the closing of the EuroLeague players’ market, ensuring that fans will have a clear picture of each team’s final roster.

All fans,  except those who have already purchased tickets during previous sales phases or pre-sales , will be eligible to access the February 26 general public sale starting at 10:00 CET. During this second sales window,  all fans will also have the opportunity to purchase VIP tickets starting at 14:00 CET .

Ticketing information is available at [https://euroleaguef4tickets.com/en/](https://euroleaguef4tickets.com/en/). Fans must log in or create a EuroLeague ID account to complete their purchase. Once logged in, all available categories will be displayed. Due to expected high demand and to ensure fairness, each fan may purchase a maximum of two tickets.

For the first time, Final Four tickets are being released in multiple sales phases. This approach is designed to expand access for the EuroLeague’s growing global fan base, offering multiple opportunities to secure tickets,  whether fans plan to attend regardless of the qualified teams or prefer to wait until the competition reaches its decisive stages. All phases follow a fair access policy, meaning that fans who purchase tickets in one phase will not be eligible to participate in subsequent phases. This measure is designed to ensure broader access to the event.

Phase 1, held on February 11, experienced massive demand, as over a million fans connected to sold out all available tickets in less than five minutes, once again demonstrating the enormous appeal of the EuroLeague’s premier event.

The 2026 EuroLeague Final Four semifinals will be played on Friday, May 22, at 18:00 and 21:00 local time (17:00 and 20:00 CEST). On Sunday, May 24, the adidas NextGen EuroLeague Championship Game will tip off at 18:00 local time (17:00 CEST), while the season will culminate with the Championship Game at 21:00 local time (20:00 CEST), when a new EuroLeague champion will be crowned.

A third ticket sales window will open on Tuesday, March 17, at 10:00 CET, offering another opportunity for fans to secure their seats. The final sales phase will launch on Monday, April 20, at 10:00 CEST, positioned between the conclusion of the Regular Season and the start of the Play-In Showdown, with 10 teams still in contention for a place in the Final Four.

All ticketing details for the 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens presented by Etihad are available at [https://euroleaguef4tickets.com/en/](https://euroleaguef4tickets.com/en/).

articleMetadata.title: 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens presented by Etihad – Ticket sales Phase 2 to start on February 26
articleMetadata.description: Ticket sales for the 2026 EuroLeague Final Four Athens presented by Etihad will enter their next chapter on February 26 at 10:00 CET, as Phase 2 opens to the general public.

heroMedia.title: Spanish Cup: Hezonja's magic lifts Real past Valencia, Baskonia rallies against Barcelona

content.text:
Mario Hezonja hit 7 three-pointers, including a pair in the final 17 seconds of the game, lifting Real Madrid to an improbable 108-106 win over home side Valencia Basket in the Spanish Cup semifinal on Saturday. In the second game, Baskonia erased a 10-point fourth-quarter deficit to edge FC Barcelona 67-70. This is Baskonia's first appearance in the Cup finals since 2009.

### Hezonja caps Real's improbable comeback

Valencia, which hit 7 first-quarter triples and held 34-16 lead after the opening 10 minutes, let that lead slip before halftime, but then seemed to get the game under control in the fourth quarter. Behind Jean Montero, who caught fire, Valencia was up 11 points with under 6 minutes to go, and up 89-99 with 4 minutes left in the game.

Hezonja nailed his fourth three-pointer to start Real’s rally, and his fifth triple cut it to 99-101 with 1:17 to go. But Montero, who scored 14 fourth-quarter points up to that point, nailed a three-pointer that had Valencia up 101-106 with 20.1 to go.

However, Hezonja quickly hit one more three, then Montero made a mistake on an ensuing inbound play followed by Hezonja’s corner triple, his seventh of the night, to put Real up 107-106.

Valencia had one final possession, but Gabriel Deck stopped Montero’s drive to the basket, and *Los Blancos* celebrated a win that seemed unlikely just few minutes earlier.

Hezonja finished with 25 points, Facundo Campazzo had 17, while Walter Tavares and Deck each added 13 points, with Tavares also pulling down 8 rebounds. Montero ended up scoring 19 of his game-high 26 points in the fourth quarter, Kameron Taylor scored 20 while Nate Reuvers had 13 in a loss.

### Baskonia's rallies late against Barcelona

In the second semi, Baskonia also came back to reach the final. Barcelona led 61-51 in the first minute of the fourth quarter after a triple from Tomas Satoransky, but Baskonia rallied with a 2-13 run. Timothe Luwawu-Cabarrot and Rodions Kurucs each hit a triple, before Trent Forrest euro-stepped his way to the rim to give Baskonia 63-64 lead.

A three from Mamadi Diakite and a floater from Gytis Radzevicius made it 65-69 with 2:43 to go, and Baskonia would prevail even though it did not score another field goal the rest of the way.

Barcelona cut the lead with a fastbreak dunk from Shengelia, and Shengelia had a chance to tie the game with 10 seconds to go, but Diakite delivered a two-handed block and Baskonia preserved the lead.

Kurucs led Baskonia scorers with 14 points, Luwawu-Cabarrot had 13, while Forrest collected 10 points, 8 assists and 6 rebounds. Diakite had 10 points, 5 rebounds and 4 blocks. For Barcelona, Shengelia and Nico Laprovittola scored 12 apiece, while Will Clyburn scored 11.

articleMetadata.title: Spanish Cup: Hezonja's magic lifts Real past Valencia, Baskonia rallies against Barcelona
